## Sunda Trench Subduction Zone

**Which plate subducts beneath which:**
The **Indo-Australian Plate** (or more precisely the Indian/Australian plate) subducts beneath the **Eurasian Plate** (Sunda microplate/block) along the Sunda Trench (also called the Java Trench).

## Convergence Rates

The convergence rate varies significantly along the trench:

- **Western part (off Sumatra):** approximately **52–60 mm/yr**, with a significant oblique component — this obliquity is partly accommodated by the Sumatran Fault (strike-slip) running onshore.
- **Eastern part (off Java):** approximately **60–70 mm/yr**, with convergence being more **orthogonal** (perpendicular) to the trench compared to the Sumatra segment.

The direction of convergence rotates from more northeastward motion in the west to more northward/northwestward motion in the east, and the rate generally increases eastward along the Java segment.

These figures come from geodetic and plate motion model studies (e.g., NUVEL-1A, MORVEL).
eq-07.reference_answer: At the Sunda Trench off southern Java, the northward-moving Australian Plate subducts beneath the Sunda Plate. Convergence of relatively old oceanic crust occurs at a rate of 6 cm (2.4 in) per year in the western part and 4.9 cm (1.9 in) per year in the eastern part of the trench [2026 Pacitan earthquake].
